source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 8.7 Default rights and mask (pp. 154-157)

questions.q: With the usual mask, what default permissions do a new directory and a new file get?
questions.why: New directories default to 755 and new files to 644.

questions.q: What is the default UMASK value defined in /etc/login.defs?
questions.why: login.defs sets UMASK 022, which yields 755 for directories and 644 for files.

questions.q: How does the mask determine a new file's permissions?
options: It removes the masked rights from the maximum, and new files never receive the execute bit anyway
It adds the masked rights to the minimum
It sets the exact permissions directly
questions.why: The mask subtracts rights from the maximum; additionally, new files never get x, which is why a 022 mask gives files 644, not 755.
umask with no argument prints the current mask.

questions.q: Display the umask in symbolic form (as u=...,g=...,o=...).
questions.why: umask -S shows the resulting file rights symbolically (without the execute bit).

questions.q: Set the umask to 027 for the current session.
questions.why: umask <mode> sets the mask; 027 (or 077) is a common hardening choice.

questions.q: What is the scope of a umask set on the command line, and how do you make it permanent?
options: It applies only to the current session; make it permanent in profile files (/etc/profile, /etc/bashrc, ~/.bashrc)
It changes existing files immediately and is always permanent
questions.why: umask affects only the current session and does not touch existing files; put it in a profile file to persist (it overrides the login.defs UMASK).
